content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive, Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660              322-5901           222-6441              322-5444

May 10, 1983


Brian Swift
City Attorney
City of Pleasanton
P.O. Box 520
Pleasanton, CA  94566

Re: Advice No. A-83-096

Dear Mr. Swift:

Thank you for your request for advice on the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act.  Because your questions involve past reporting responsibilities rather than future filing obligations, I cannot provide you with specific advice.  However, since the questions presented seem to commonly recur, I am providing you with a general explanation of the law.

The facts, as I understand them, are that the Pleasanton City Council approved a housing development which was opposed by a citizens' group.  In response, the citizens' group qualified a ballot measure on the housing development's construction.  One of the developers of the housing development challenged the measure's qualification in court, where it was successfully extended by the citizens' group.  Once the court challenge was resolved, a group of developers began making expenditures to defeat the ballot measure, including hiring a consultant.  The issues presented are:

     (1)  Do groups which receive money and expend funds to qualify a ballot measure, to defend its qualification, or to promote or defeat its passage have reporting responsibilities?

     (2)  Does an individual or entity which independently expends funds for legal fees to challenge the qualification of a ballot measure have a reporting obligation?

     (3)  When should expenditures for attorney's fees and consulting fees be reported?

Brian Swift
May 10, 1983
Page 2

In response to the first question, any group which receives
$500 or more in contributions is a recipient committee under
Government Code Section 82013(a).1/ A recipient committee
must report all of its receipts and expenditures.2/

A "contribution" is defined as a payment made for political
purposes. This definition includes payments which are made to a
committee for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the
qualification or passage of any measure, if adequate
consideration is not received by the donor. (2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18223(a)(1).)

In the Buchanan Opinion, No. 78-013, 5 FPPC Opinions 14,
15-16, the term "contributions" was construed to include
payments made for certain litigation expenses. The Opinion
stated that:

Although payments for the cost of litigation are not
generally thought of as having any connections with
political campaigns ... litigation costs ... [can be]
... just as key to the success of the campaign as
traditional campaign costs such as mailings and media
advertisements. When expenditures are made to support
litigation aimed at gaining a place on the ballot for a
candidate or a measure, aimed at keeping a candidate or
measure off the ballot, or challenging the results of
an election, the expenditures are made for the purpose
of influencing the outcome of the election in favor of
or against a particular candidate or measure and should
be reported....

Thus, if a group receives over $500 in payments to qualify a
ballot measure, to defend its qualification against a legal
challenge, or to promote or defeat its passage, the group
qualifies as a recipient committee. As a recipient committee,
it must report all of its receipts and expenditures on the form
420.

The second question presented involves a situation where an
individual or entity receives no contributions and only makes

1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

2/ Recipient committees are required to register with the
Secretary of State's Office.

Brian Swift
May 10, 1983
Page 3


payments that are not to, or at the direction and control of, a
candidate or committee. Under these circumstances, the
individual or entity has a reporting obligation only if it
qualifies as an "independent expenditure committee." To qualify
as an "independent expenditure committee," an individual or
entity must make $500 or more in expenditures that are:

     ... in connection with a communication which expressly
advocates the passage or defeat of a clearly identified
ballot measure, or taken as a whole and in context,
unambiguously urge a particular result in an election
but which ... [are] not made to or at the behest of the
affected candidate or committee.

Payments for attorney's fees incurred in legally challenging
the qualification of a ballot measure meet the definition of
"independent expenditures." First, they are "expenditures"
because they are payments made for "political purposes" (see the
earlier discussion of the Buchanan Opinion),2/ and, secondly,
the payments are made in connection with a communication (the
court challenge) which expressly advocates the defeat of a
measure and which unambiguously urge the measure's defeat in an
election.

     Once $500 or more in attorney's fees are paid, the
individual or entity must file as an independent expenditure
committee and report these, and future, payments on forms 461
and 465.

     With regard to the final question, a committee must disclose
expenditures for attorney's fees and consulting fees if, at the
time the committee files its report, it is aware of the amount
of the expenditure. (For example, if an advance fee payment was
made.)  However, if, on the filing date, the committee does not
know the amount of the expenditures incurred to date (for
instance, if an attorney or consultant is working on an hourly
basis), then the committee reports the expenditures as of the
last monthly billing.1/

     2/ Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(a) defines
"expenditure" as any monetary or nonmonetary payment made for
political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is
not made to the donor.

     1/ Billings must be on at least a monthly basis.

Brian Swift
May 10, 1983
Page 4


I hope I have answered all of your questions. If I can be
of any further help to you, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh
cc: Frederick Lowell
    Ron Schnieder

LAW OFFICES OF

F P P C NORMOYLE & NEWMAN
A PROFESSIONAL LAW CORPORATION

[illegible] 12 [illegible] J 11700 STANDIFORD AVENUE - SUITE A-340
MODESTO, CALIFORNIA 95350

MICHAEL C. NORMOYLE TELEPHONE (209) 521-9521 TELECOPIER (209) 521-4968 DAVID ROMANO, P.E.
RUSSELL A. NEWMAN MICHAEL S. WARDA
WYLIE P. CASHMAN Land Use Analysis
JOHN T. RESSO
PATRICK M. McGRATH

February 24, 1994

Ms. Linda Moureaux
Technical Assistance &
Analyst Division of
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Payment for Qualification of Referendum

Dear Ms. Moureaux:

This letter is in confirmation of our conversation today where you outlined the
reporting requirements of a situation I described. A corporation has paid an amount in excess
of $1,000.00 to a company to print petitions and gather signatures for a referendum in a
California county. The corporation has not received contributions for this effort, but paid the
entire amount itself.

You informed me that the corporation qualified as a Major Donor Committee and
would file Form 461 on a semi-annual basis. You further stated that should the corporation
expend funds in the future for passage of the referendum, it may qualify as a Independent
Expenditure Committee and thus file Form 465. The committee has no immediate filing
requirements. For example, it need not file Form 410 within the 10 day period provided for that
form.

You were kind enough to put in the mail to me today Information Manual E:
Independent Expenditure Committees and Major Donor Committees as well as the relevant forms.

February 24, 1994
Page 2


Please inform me if my understanding in this letter is incorrect in any way. Thank you for your assistance today.


Very truly yours,
NORMOYLE & NEWMAN

[signature]
George K. Petrulakis


GAP/cmf

cc:     Mark Palmer
        Steven Herum

g\cr\moureaux.f24

sections.question: (1) Do groups which receive money and expend funds to qualify a ballot measure, to defend its qualification, or to promote or defeat its passage have reporting responsibilities? (2) Does an individual or entity which independently expends funds for legal fees to challenge the qualification of a ballot measure have a reporting obligation? (3) When should expenditures for attorney's fees and consulting fees be reported?
sections.conclusion: Any group receiving $500 or more in contributions for ballot measure qualification, defense, or passage/defeat must register as a recipient committee and report on Form 420. Individuals or entities making $500+ in independent expenditures to challenge a measure's qualification (including legal fees) must file as an independent expenditure committee on Forms 461 and 465. Committees must disclose attorney and consulting fees based on amounts known at filing time, or report as of the last monthly billing if amounts are unknown.
sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses three interrelated questions about campaign reporting obligations under California's Political Reform Act: whether citizen groups that receive contributions to qualify, defend, or promote/defeat ballot measures must report; whether individuals or entities independently spending money to challenge a measure's qualification must report; and when attorney and consulting fees should be reported.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that campaign reporting obligations apply to both groups receiving contributions for ballot measure activities (as recipient committees) and individuals/entities independently spending $500+ to challenge measure qualification (as independent expenditure committees). Attorney and consulting fees constitute reportable expenditures when tied to electoral purposes, and must be disclosed based on known amounts or latest monthly billing.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Primary document is the May 10, 1983 advice letter (A-83-096) to Brian Swift. A secondary document (Feb 24, 1994 correspondence from Normoyle & Newman confirming a phone conversation with FPPC staff) is appended but appears to be a confirmation letter rather than an FPPC response letter. Extraction focuses on the primary advice letter which contains clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. The 1983 letter references the Buchanan Opinion (78-013) and applies it to the specific facts presented.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Do groups which receive money and expend funds to qualify a ballot measure, to defend its qualification, or to promote or defeat its passage have reporting responsibilities? (2) Does an individual or entity which independently expends funds for legal fees to challenge the qualification of a ballot measure have a reporting obligation? (3) When should expenditures for attorney's fees and consulting fees be reported?

CONCLUSION: Any group receiving $500 or more in contributions for ballot measure qualification, defense, or passage/defeat must register as a recipient committee and report on Form 420. Individuals or entities making $500+ in independent expenditures to challenge a measure's qualification (including legal fees) must file as an independent expenditure committee on Forms 461 and 465. Committees must disclose attorney and consulting fees based on amounts known at filing time, or report as of the last monthly billing if amounts are unknown.

embedding.summary: In this May 10, 1983 advice letter responding to the City Attorney of Pleasanton, the FPPC addresses campaign reporting obligations for groups involved in ballot measures, including those receiving contributions to qualify measures and those independently spending funds for litigation challenging a measure's qualification. The letter clarifies that such groups qualify as either recipient committees or independent expenditure committees with specific Form filing requirements.
